# Crux corpus — files, schema & subtopics

The crux corpus is a retrieval bank of the load-bearing moves of solved pre-2026
Olympiad problems (disjoint from the 2026 test set), stored as two JSON arrays
joined by `problem_id`:

- `past_crux_moves_database.json` — the load-bearing move of each solved pre-2026
  problem. **2434 cruxes** across three domains: number_theory (548),
  combinatorics (1114), algebra (772).
- `past_problems_database.json` — the full statement + solution(s) of those
  problems (1026 problems; covers every `problem_id` referenced by a crux).

**Use the exact field names below. Do not guess** — `crux_move` and `statement` do
**not** exist and querying them returns blank content.

## `past_crux_moves_database.json` fields

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `problem_id` | join key |
| `technique` | the crux move — the name of the load-bearing idea |
| `how_used` | how that move cracked the problem |
| `domain` | one of `number_theory`, `combinatorics`, `algebra`; use this to filter first |
| `subtopic` | one subtopic (see list below); use this to filter within a domain |
| `technique_tags`, `subtopics`, `retries` | secondary metadata |

## `past_problems_database.json` fields

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `problem_id` | join key |
| `problem` | the full statement (**not** `statement` / `problem_statement`) |
| `solutions` | the full solution(s) |
| `domain`, `competition`, `exam`, `year`, `problem_label`, `problem_type` | metadata |

## Subtopics by domain

Each crux carries one `domain` and one `subtopic`. Filter by `domain` first, then
by these `subtopic` values (listed most-common first). Match the strings exactly.

**number_theory** (548 cruxes) —
`size-bounding-and-descent` · `divisibility-and-gcd` ·
`modular-arithmetic-and-CRT` · `p-adic-valuation` · `diophantine-and-factoring` ·
`orders-and-primitive-roots` · `lifting-the-exponent` · `vieta-jumping` ·
`cyclotomic-and-roots-of-unity` · `polynomial-roots-and-factoring` ·
`sequences-and-recurrences` · `telescoping-and-summation` · `double-counting` ·
`functional-equations` · `graph-theory-and-connectivity` ·
`induction-and-construction` · `pigeonhole` · `zsigmondy-and-primitive-divisors` ·
`invariants-and-monovariants` · `coloring-and-parity` · `games-and-strategy`

**combinatorics** (1114 cruxes) —
`induction-and-construction` · `invariants-and-monovariants` ·
`extremal-principle` · `double-counting` · `bijections-and-encoding` ·
`coloring-and-parity` · `graph-theory-and-connectivity` ·
`processes-and-algorithms` · `pigeonhole` · `games-and-strategy` ·
`linear-algebra-method` · `generating-functions` · `orders-and-primitive-roots` ·
`telescoping-and-summation` · `cyclotomic-and-roots-of-unity` ·
`inequalities-SOS-and-convexity` · `probabilistic-method` ·
`size-bounding-and-descent` · `modular-arithmetic-and-CRT` ·
`sequences-and-recurrences` · `diophantine-and-factoring` · `divisibility-and-gcd` ·
`p-adic-valuation`

**algebra** (772 cruxes) —
`functional-equations` · `i...

input_data.prompt:
Scout the crux of IMO 2026 Problem 6. Write your report to `/tmp/round-3/math-explorer-wqo-repair.md`.

## The problem
Let a_1 < a_2 < ... be integers > 1 where a_{n+1} = smallest integer > a_n sharing a prime with every a_1,...,a_n. Prove ∃T,L: a_{n+T} = a_n + L for all n ≥ 1.

## State of play
Two partial approaches exist (read `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/greedy-clique-closure.md` and `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/small-prime-core.md`). Both reduce to the same crux:

**Why are only finitely many primes "essential" for the constraint structure?**

Both approaches have the same gap but attack it differently:
1. `greedy-clique-closure`: tries density/first-moment to show E_∞ = {minimal prime sets of terms} is finite
2. `small-prime-core`: tries to show ALL edges of the clique are covered by primes ≤ B = rad(a_1)

## Your mission: attack these two specific strategies

**Strategy A - The "Small Common Prime Lemma" of small-prime-core**:
CLAIM: For any two terms x < y, gcd(x,y) has a prime factor ≤ B (where B = rad(a_1)).

Is this true? Try to:
1. Verify or find a counterexample with explicit small a_1 values (e.g., a_1 = 6, 10, 15, 30, 35).
2. If true, prove it. Key inputs: x and y share a prime (clique). The shared prime p divides gcd(x,y). Why must p ≤ B?

Proof attempt: When y = a_k was chosen, every integer in (a_{k-1}, a_k) was invalid. The term a_{k-1} ≥ a_1 and a_k ≤ a_{k-1} + B. Can we show: if p | a_k and p > B is prime, then the "essential" constraint that p helps satisfy is already satisfied by the small-prime part of a_k?

Key: Let a_k = p * c (with p > B prime). For a_k to be valid, for every earlier term a_j, gcd(a_k, a_j) > 1. This is satisfied if either p | a_j OR some other prime q | gcd(a_k, a_j). If p | a_j for some j < k, then p is "shared" (appears in the sequence before a_k). But p > B, so a_j is divisible by a prime p > B > rad(a_1); since a_j shares a prime with a_1, a_j must also have a prime in P(a_1). So a_j has BOTH a small prime and p. Now when a_k came along, it could have used the small prime of a_j instead...

This direction seems promising. Scout it carefully.

**Strategy B - WQO repair via descent**:
The standard WQO argument fails (P_fin(primes) under ⊆ is NOT WQO). But: every non-dominated term a_{n_k} has P(a_{n_k}) ∩ P(a_1) ≠ ∅ (since every term shares a prime with a_1). So we can partition the infinitely-many (assumed) non-dominated terms by which prime in P(a_1) = {q_1,...,q_r} they contain: pigeonhole gives infinitely many sharing some fixed q ∈ P(a_1).

Among those sharing q: consider the sequence P'(a_{n_{k_j}}) = P(a_{n_{k_j}}) \ {q}. These form a bad sequence in P_fin(primes \ {q}). Repeat: partition by which prime in P(a_1) \ {q} they contain (they must share a prime with ALL earlier terms, in particular with the q-free ones). This gives another infinite subfamily sharing a second prime from P(a_1). But P(a_1) is finite — after |P(a_1)| steps of descent, we've removed all primes of P(a_1) from the ...

file.content:
## Goal

**Problem:** imo-2026-06

**Statement:** Let $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ be an infinite sequence of positive integers greater than 1. Suppose that for all positive integers $n$, the number $a_{n+1}$ is the smallest positive integer greater than $a_n$ such that $\gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1$ for every $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. Prove that there exist positive integers $T$ and $L$ such that $a_{n+T} = a_n + L$ for every positive integer n.

**Task:** proof_only (no answer to verify, just prove the statement)

**Metric:** proof correctness (APPROVE from proof-reviewer)

**Eval command:** Read results/imo-2026-06/current.md - Status solved = goal achieved

**Baseline:** Status = unsolved (no proof attempts yet)

**Target:** Status = solved (complete, rigorous proof with proof-reviewer APPROVE)

**Constraint:** All rigor rules from CLAUDE.md must be satisfied

## Goal Updates

## Eval History

## Rules
- ALWAYS read knowledge_base.md and crux_moves before generating new approaches (round 1)
- NEVER split one proof across slugs -- each slug is a complete attempt at the whole problem
- ALWAYS diversify approaches: different framings, not just different techniques
- ALWAYS run outline-reviewer every round regardless of perceived progress

## State

**Done:**
- Round 1: Setup complete. Problem identified as imo-2026-06 (IMO P6 2026, difficulty 9/10). Workspace created at results/imo-2026-06/. Packages installed.

**Broken:**
(none)

**Next:**
- Run math-explorers (parallel, different lenses) -> proof-outliner -> outline-reviewer -> proof-builders -> proof-reviewer

ALWAYS: Run Python experiments early on sequences/combinatorics problems to discover the numerical structure before thinking about proofs (because experimental data reveals the right conjecture faster than pure thought, round 1)

ALWAYS: Verify periodicity experiments by checking a_{n+T} = a_n + L for n in a range of indices, not just a few (because periodic patterns can have a transient that confuses small-sample checks, round 1)

ALWAYS: For greedy sequence problems, compute the stable residue set mod L and verify it matches a simple number-theoretic characterization (because this reveals the right proof structure, round 1)

NEVER: Assume the stable prime set S equals the prime factors of a_1; S depends on the full dynamics and can both add and drop primes (round 1)

ALWAYS: Verify the periodicity statement holds for ALL n (not just eventually) — for IMO-style "a_{n+T}=a_n+L for all n" problems, this global version is often what's claimed and follows from a bijective state-machine argument, not just eventual periodicity (round 1, imo-2026-06)

ALWAYS: For greedy gcd-sequences, check the CLIQUE PROPERTY first (any two terms share a prime) — this is immediate from the definition and the key organizing lemma (round 1, imo-2026-06)

ALWAYS: Track when V mod M stabilizes (not when the prime set stabilizes) — the prime set may never stabilize but V mod M can stabilize after just a few terms (round 1, imo-2026-06)

NEVER: Assume the prime set of a greedy gcd-sequence is finite — new primes keep appearing in later terms as bonus factors, but their constraints are redundant mod M (round 1, imo-2026-06)

ALWAYS: For antichain-stabilization in greedy sequences, use the WQO (Dickson's lemma) applied to the SUBsequence of non-dominated terms — if P(a_i) ⊆ P(a_j) for i<j, the domination lemma shows a_j is dominated; so non-dominated prime sets form a bad sequence → finite by Dickson (round 2, imo-2026-06)

NEVER: Claim "E_n self-consistent iff E_n has a singleton" — counterexample: E* = {{2,3},{2,5},{3,5}} is self-consistent (every valid m divisible by ≥2 of {2,3,5}) with no singleton (round 2, imo-2026-06)

ALWAYS: Verify the "global for all n≥1" vs "eventual for n≥N" periodicity distinction in greedy sequence problems — the global version often requires showing all terms (including early ones) are already in V*, the stable valid set (round 2, imo-2026-06)

## Status
partial

## Approach outline

**Framing.** Work with the *limit object*: the fixed set $V_\infty = \{m > 1 : \gcd(m, a_i) > 1 \text{ for all } i \ge 1\}$ and the set of terms $A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots\}$. Show the sequence is exactly the increasing enumeration of $V_\infty \cap [a_1, \infty)$, then show $V_\infty$ is an *exactly periodic* set (a union of residue classes mod some $L$), which yields $a_{n+T} = a_n + L$ for **every** $n \ge 1$ (no transient — verified computationally for $a_1 = 15, 35, 77, 91, 105$).

The route: Clique → Bounded gaps → Reduction Lemma → Finite essential antichain (the hard gap, attacked by a density/first-moment argument) → Exact periodicity of $V_\infty$ → Greedy on a periodic set.

### Step 1: Clique Lemma
Any two distinct terms satisfy $\gcd(a_m, a_n) > 1$. — Immediate from the definition: for $m > n$, $a_m$ was chosen with $\gcd(a_m, a_i) > 1$ for all $i < m$, in particular $i = n$. (Provable in two lines; candidate for `lemmas/clique.md`.)

### Step 2: Universal validity of multiples, bounded gaps
For **every** term $a$ of the sequence, every multiple $m$ of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ (with $m > 1$) satisfies $\gcd(m, a_i) > 1$ for all $i$. — Mechanism: by Step 1, $a_i$ shares some prime $p$ with $a$; then $p \mid \mathrm{rad}(a) \mid m$, so $\gcd(m, a_i) \ge p > 1$. Consequently, at every stage the next multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a_1)$ is a valid candidate, so
$$a_{n+1} - a_n \le B := \mathrm{rad}(a_1) \quad \text{for all } n,$$
and hence $A$ has lower density $\ge 1/B$ in every long interval: $|A \cap [x, x+y]| \ge \lfloor y/B \rfloor$. Moreover every multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ that is $\ge a_1$ is itself a **term** (by Step 3). (Provable; candidate for `lemmas/bounded-gaps.md`.)

### Step 3: Reduction Lemma (the sequence enumerates $V_\infty$)
$a_{n+1} = \min\{m \in V_\infty : m > a_n\}$ for every $n$; consequently $A = V_\infty \cap [a_1, \infty)$ and the sequence is its increasing enumeration. — Mechanism: (i) every term lies in $V_\infty$: for $i < k$, $\gcd(a_k, a_i) > 1$ by definition of $a_k$; for $i > k$, by definition of $a_i$; and $\gcd(a_k, a_k) > 1$. (ii) Let $V_n = \{m > a_n : \gcd(m, a_i) > 1,\ i \le n\}$; then $V_\infty \cap (a_n, \infty) \subseteq V_n$, so $\min V_\infty \cap (a_n,\infty) \ge \min V_n = a_{n+1}$; but $a_{n+1} \in V_\infty \cap (a_n, \infty)$ by (i), forcing equality. Also $a_1 \in V_\infty$. (Provable; candidate for `lemmas/reduction.md`. This kills all worry about "old constraints being forgotten" — the constraint set is the fixed infinite family $\{\mathrm{primes}(a_i)\}_{i \ge 1}$.)

### Step 4: Finite essential antichain — **[GAP: the crux]**
Let $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathrm{primes}(a_i) : i \ge 1\}$ and let $E_\infty$ be the set of inclusion-minimal members of $\mathcal{F}$. Since each member is a finite set, every member contains a minimal member (take an inclusion-minimal member among the finitely many members contained in it), so
$$V_\infty = \{m > 1 : \mathrm{primes}(m) \...

## Status
partial

## Approach outline

**Framing.** Fix a *finite prime universe in advance* and show all the action happens inside it. Let $Q$ be a finite set of small primes (first candidate: all primes $\le B := \mathrm{rad}(a_1)$; the proof may need a larger explicit threshold) and $M = \prod_{p \in Q} p$. The single structural claim driving everything: **any two terms of the sequence share a prime belonging to $Q$** (equivalently: no clique edge is carried only by large primes). Granting this, every gcd-constraint collapses to a condition on residues mod $M$, membership of the term set $A$ becomes a condition on $m \bmod M$ alone, and exact periodicity falls out with $L = M$ (or a divisor). No antichain-stabilization-over-time analysis is needed at all — the finite poset $2^Q$ does that work for free.

This is a genuinely different route from `greedy-clique-closure`: there the finiteness of the minimal constraint family is the target and primes can a priori be arbitrarily large; here we pin the prime universe first and never form the antichain.

Computational support: for $a_1 \in \{15, 35, 77, 91, 105\}$ (150 terms each), *every* pair of terms shares a prime $\le$ the largest prime factor of $a_1$ — no pair ever relies on a large prime.

### Step 1: Clique and bounded gaps (shared scaffolding)
(i) $\gcd(a_m, a_n) > 1$ for all $m \neq n$ — by definition of the greedy rule, both directions.
(ii) For every term $a$, every multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ exceeding 1 is a valid candidate at every stage (it shares with each $a_i$ the prime that $a_i$ shares with $a$); hence $a_{n+1} - a_n \le B = \mathrm{rad}(a_1)$, and every multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ that is $\ge a_1$ is itself a term (using Step 2(i) below).

### Step 2: Reduction Lemma
With $V_\infty = \{m > 1 : \gcd(m, a_i) > 1\ \forall i\}$: (i) every term lies in $V_\infty$, and (ii) $a_{n+1} = \min V_\infty \cap (a_n, \infty)$, so $A := \{a_n\} = V_\infty \cap [a_1, \infty)$, enumerated increasingly. — Mechanism: $V_\infty \subseteq V_n$ gives $\ge$; $a_{n+1} \in V_\infty$ gives $\le$. (Identical to `greedy-clique-closure` Step 3; certify once in `lemmas/` and import.)

### Step 3: Small common prime lemma — **[GAP: the crux]**
**Claim:** there is a finite set of primes $Q$ (explicitly bounded in terms of $a_1$) such that for all terms $x \neq y$, $\gcd(x, y)$ has a prime factor in $Q$.

A sufficient weaker form (also acceptable): only *finitely many pairs* of terms share exclusively primes $> B$; then enlarge $Q$ by the finitely many large primes involved.

Attack plan for the builder:
- Suppose $x < y$ are terms with $\gcd(x, y)$ supported on primes $> z$ only, and let $q > z$ be a common prime, so $q \le x$, i.e. both $x, y \in q\mathbb{Z}$ with $q$ large.
- Use greedy minimality of $y$: when $y$ was chosen (say $y = a_{k+1}$), every integer in $(a_k, y)$ failed some constraint. The window $(a_k, y)$ has length $< B$ (Step 1(ii)). So the "reason $y$ won" is local, while $y$ carries t...

# Olympiad Knowledge Base

Theorems and techniques for hard (difficulty-8) olympiad problems across algebra,
number theory, combinatorics, geometry, and linear algebra. Organized by technique,
not by problem domain — many problems mix areas.

## Algebra & Polynomials

- **Vieta's formulas**: roots ↔ coefficients. For systems of symmetric equations,
  treat unknowns as roots of a polynomial and read off symmetric functions.
- **Symmetric functions & Newton's identities**: convert between power sums
  `p_k = Σ xᵢᵏ` and elementary symmetric polynomials. Use to match power-sum equalities.
- **Resultants / "transform the roots"**: to show `P(r)=0 ⟹ Q(f(r))=0`, exhibit a
  polynomial identity `g(x)·P(x) = Q(f(x))`; then `P(r)=0` forces `Q(f(r))=0`.
- **Minimal-polynomial reduction**: given `P(x)=0`, reduce any high power `xᵏ` by
  repeatedly substituting the relation (e.g. `x³ = 8 − 4x`) until degree `< deg P`.
- **Sum of squares (SOS) / completing the square**: prove a real inequality by
  rewriting `LHS − RHS` as a sum of squares; equivalently a positive-semidefinite
  quadratic form.
- **Piecewise-concavity smoothing**: to minimize a function `g(φ)` that is a finite
  sum of terms each of the form `|single sinusoid|` (i.e. `|A cos(kφ)+B sin(kφ)|`),
  note each such term is concave on every interval between two consecutive zeros of
  its argument (an identically-zero term is constant, hence concave). A finite sum of
  functions concave on a common subinterval is concave there. Partitioning the circle
  by the (finitely many) zeros of all arguments gives a piecewise-concave `g`; a
  concave function on a closed interval attains its min at an endpoint, so the global
  min of `g` is at a breakpoint, where some sinusoid argument vanishes. If the term is
  a product `|x_φ · y|`, vanishing forces a factor to vanish — a "zero entry."
  Moreover, if `φ=0` is already a global minimizer and lies in the interior of a
  concavity interval, concavity forces `g` constant on that interval, so its endpoints
  are also global minimizers carrying an extra zero entry. Used to drive a minimizer to
  a sparse (axial) configuration without raising the objective.
- **Standard inequalities**: AM-GM, Cauchy-Schwarz, QM-AM, Schur. Equality cases
  pin down the extremal configuration.
- **Functional equations**: test special values, check injectivity/surjectivity.
- **Complex roots & moduli**: `|root product| = |constant term|`; conjugate
  symmetry (`b = ā`) and `aⁿ = bⁿ` cases for modulus conditions.

## Linear Algebra

- **Quadratic forms**: a symmetric form is PSD iff it's a sum of squares iff all

- **Symmetric matrices**: real symmetric ⟹ orthogonally diagonalizable, real
  eigenvalues. `A²` is PSD; `Σ Aᵢ²` singular ⟺ the `Aᵢ` share a common null vector.
- **Rank / image / kernel**: `det(Σ Aᵢ Bᵢ) = 0 ∀Bᵢ` ⟺ the `Aᵢ` have a common

from math import prod

def rad(n):
    return prod(factorint(n).keys())

    return set(factorint(n).keys())

            valid = all(bool(pf.intersection(ps)) for ps in primes_in_terms)

                primes_in_terms.append(pf)

--- Solution 1 ---
Let us first observe that the number appearing on the blackboard decreases after every move; so the game necessarily ends after at most $n$ steps, and consequently there always has to be some player possessing a winning strategy. So if some $n \geqslant k$ is bad, then Ana has a winning strategy in the game with starting number $n$. ## imo-2026-06

### What I found

Both existing approaches (greedy-clique-closure and small-prime-core) share a common gap: proving "large prime factors of terms are irrelevant to the essential constraint structure." The gap appears as either "E_∞ is finite" or "all pairs of terms share a prime ≤ B." This is NOT intractable — I found a complete proof route.

---

### BREAKTHROUGH: Complete proof of the Small Common Prime Lemma

**Lemma (SCPL):** Any two distinct terms a_i, a_j (i < j) share a prime factor ≤ B = rad(a_1).

**Proof sketch (induction on j, descent on i):**

**Base j=2:** a_1 and a_2 share a prime in primes(a_1) ⊆ {p ≤ B}. ✓

**Inductive step:** Assume for all j' < j, every pair (a_{i'}, a_{j'}) with i' < j' shares a prime ≤ B. Want: every (a_i, a_j) with i < j shares a prime ≤ B.

Suppose not. Take i MINIMAL so that (a_i, a_j) shares only large primes (> B). Then for all i' < i: (a_{i'}, a_j) shares a small prime.

**Key tool — Claim 4 (aimo-0030 analog):** For any term a with large prime q > B, let σ(a) = primes(a) ∩ {p ≤ B} ≠ ∅. Then there exists an integer x_a ≥ a_1 with primes(x_a) = σ(a) and x_a < a. (Construction: x_a = p^{n_0} · ∏_{r ∈ σ(a), r≠p} r with n_0 minimal so x_a ≥ a_1; then x_a < a because removing the large prime q gives x_a < a/q · something ≤ a. Verified computationally for a_1 ∈ {15,35,77,30}.)

**Key structural fact:** V_{k-1} ∩ (a_{k-1}, a_k) = ∅ for all k ≥ 1. [By greedy: a_k = min V_{k-1} ∩ (a_{k-1}, ∞).] Consequence: V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1, ..., a_{i-1}}. [Every valid element below a_i must equal some term.]

**Construct x_j** (Claim 4 applied to a_j, which has large prime q > B since a_i, a_j share only q):
- primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) ⊆ {p ≤ B}, and x_j < a_j, x_j ≥ a_1.
- x_j ∈ V_{i-1}: for each i' < i, pair (a_{i'}, a_j) shares a small prime (by i-minimality) ⟹ σ(a_j) ∩ primes(a_{i'}) ≠ ∅ ⟹ gcd(x_j, a_{i'}) > 1 for all i' ≤ i-1. ✓

**Case 1: x_j < a_i.** Then x_j ∈ V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1, ..., a_{i-1}}. So x_j = a_s for some s < i. But gcd(a_s, a_i) = gcd(x_j, a_i) = 1 (since primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) and σ(a_j) ∩ primes(a_i) = ∅ by the large-prime-only assumption). This contradicts the clique property. ✓

**Case 2: x_j ≥ a_i.** By j-inductive hypothesis applied to pairs with second index i < j: ALL pairs (a_{i'}, a_i) with i' < i share small primes. So σ(a_i) ∩ primes(a_{i'}) ≠ ∅ for all i' < i.

Also a_i has large prime q (since q | a_i by assumption). So **Construct x_i** (Claim 4 applied to a_i):
- primes(x_i) = σ(a_i), x_i < a_i, x_i ≥ a_1.
- x_i ∈ V_{i-1}: for each i' < i, pair (a_{i'}, a_i) shares a small prime ⟹ gcd(x_i, a_{i'}) > 1. ✓
- By the structural fact: x_i ∈ V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1,...,a_{i-1}}. So x_i = a_s for some s ≤ i-1.

Now: gcd(x_j, a_s) = gcd(x_j, x_i). primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) and primes(x_i) = σ(a_i), and σ(a_j) ∩ σ(a_i) = ∅ (assumption). So gcd(x_j, a_s) = 1.

But x_j ∈ V_{i-1} requires gcd(x_j, a_s) > 1 (since s ≤ i-1). CONTRADICTION. ✓

**Key tool — Claim 4 (aimo-0030 analog):** For any term a with large prime q > B, let σ(a) = primes(a) ∩ {p ≤ B} ≠ ∅. Then there exists an integer x_a ≥ a_1 with primes(x_a) = σ(a) and x_a < a. (Construction: x_a = p^{n_0} · ∏_{r ∈ σ(a), r≠p} r with n_0 minimal so x_a ≥ a_1; then x_a < a because removing the large prime q gives x_a < a/q · something ≤ a. Verified computationally for a_1 ∈ {15,35,77,30}.)

**Key structural fact:** V_{k-1} ∩ (a_{k-1}, a_k) = ∅ for all k ≥ 1. [By greedy: a_k = min V_{k-1} ∩ (a_{k-1}, ∞).] Consequence: V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1, ..., a_{i-1}}. [Every valid element below a_i must equal some term.]

**Construct x_j** (Claim 4 applied to a_j, which has large prime q > B since a_i, a_j share only q):
- primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) ⊆ {p ≤ B}, and x_j < a_j, x_j ≥ a_1.
- x_j ∈ V_{i-1}: for each i' < i, pair (a_{i'}, a_j) shares a small prime (by i-minimality) ⟹ σ(a_j) ∩ primes(a_{i'}) ≠ ∅ ⟹ gcd(x_j, a_{i'}) > 1 for all i' ≤ i-1. ✓

**Case 1: x_j < a_i.** Then x_j ∈ V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1, ..., a_{i-1}}. So x_j = a_s for some s < i. But gcd(a_s, a_i) = gcd(x_j, a_i) = 1 (since primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) and σ(a_j) ∩ primes(a_i) = ∅ by the large-prime-only assumption). This contradicts the clique property. ✓

**Case 2: x_j ≥ a_i.** By j-inductive hypothesis applied to pairs with second index i < j: ALL pairs (a_{i'}, a_i) with i' < i share small primes. So σ(a_i) ∩ primes(a_{i'}) ≠ ∅ for all i' < i.

Also a_i has large prime q (since q | a_i by assumption). So **Construct x_i** (Claim 4 applied to a_i):
- primes(x_i) = σ(a_i), x_i < a_i, x_i ≥ a_1.
- x_i ∈ V_{i-1}: for each i' < i, pair (a_{i'}, a_i) shares a small prime ⟹ gcd(x_i, a_{i'}) > 1. ✓
- By the structural fact: x_i ∈ V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1,...,a_{i-1}}. So x_i = a_s for some s ≤ i-1.

Now: gcd(x_j, a_s) = gcd(x_j, x_i). primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) and primes(x_i) = σ(a_i), and σ(a_j) ∩ σ(a_i) = ∅ (assumption). So gcd(x_j, a_s) = 1.

But x_j ∈ V_{i-1} requires gcd(x_j, a_s) > 1 (since s ≤ i-1). CONTRADICTION. ✓

output_data.prompt:
Scout alternative framings for IMO 2026 Problem 6. Write your report to `/tmp/round-3/math-explorer-alternative-routes.md`.

## The problem
Let a_1 < a_2 < ... be integers > 1 where a_{n+1} is the smallest integer > a_n sharing a prime with every a_1,...,a_n. Prove ∃T,L such that a_{n+T} = a_n + L for all n ≥ 1.

## What is established
- All pairs gcd(a_i, a_j) > 1 (clique).
- a_{n+1} - a_n ≤ B := rad(a_1); bounded gaps.
- The sequence = greedy enumeration of V_∞ = {m > 1: gcd(m,a_i) > 1 ∀i} starting from a_1.
- V_∞ is periodic mod L iff the set of minimal prime sets E_∞ is finite — THIS IS THE HARD PART that existing approaches struggle with.

## Your mission
Scout for approaches that BYPASS the "E_∞ is finite" challenge, or attack the problem from fundamentally different framings.

**Alternative 1 - Finite state argument directly on the sequence**: 
The sequence is determined by which residue classes mod various primes are "obligated". Can we model the sequence as a finite-state machine without going through antichain E_∞? E.g., track only "which primes MUST divide the next term" as a set of constraints that is finite from the start.

**Alternative 2 - Strong induction / structural argument**:
Since a_1 is fixed, what is the constraint structure at step n precisely? Is there a direct argument (not through antichain theory) showing that after some step N (depending only on a_1), the sequence becomes purely periodic?

**Alternative 3 - Look at the prime structure of a_1 more carefully**:
If a_1 = p_1^{e_1} · ... · p_k^{e_k}, then rad(a_1) = p_1 · ... · p_k. The sequence is confined: every term must share a prime with a_1, so every term has a prime in {p_1,...,p_k}. Can we build the ENTIRE proof using only these k primes? 

Sub-question: Is it true that for any two terms a_m, a_n > a_1, they share a prime from {p_1,...,p_k} (the prime factors of a_1)? If yes, this immediately gives us a finite prime universe and the rest is easy. If no, what is a counterexample?

**Alternative 4 - Direct characterization of V_∞**:
V_∞ = {m > 1: gcd(m, a_i) > 1 ∀i} = {m > 1: for every F ∈ E_∞, ∃p ∈ F with p|m}. Can we characterize V_∞ directly as "m shares a prime with a_1 AND m shares a prime with a_2 AND ..."? Does this collapse to a finite-state condition without proving E_∞ is finite?

**Alternative 5 - Special structure of the greedy sequence**:
After the sequence has seen terms sharing various primes, what does the FUTURE of the sequence look like? Specifically: can we prove that the sequence eventually becomes L-periodic for SOME L, without identifying L in advance? And then "eventually" becomes "globally" via the Reduction Lemma (already proved)?

**Alternative 6 - Algebraic/combinatorial structure**:
View the problem in terms of the hypergraph where vertices are primes and hyperedges are prime sets of terms. The "validity" of a future term is a 2-coloring constraint. Is there an algebraic structure (e.g., group-theoretic or lattice-theoretic) that forces p...

python3 -c "
from sympy import factorint
from math import prod

def rad(n):
    return prod(factorint(n).keys())

def prime_factors(n):
    return set(factorint(n).keys())

# Verify B^N argument: show that B^N in V_inf (every term shares prime with B^N)
# This is obvious: every term shares a prime with a1 (clique), and B = rad(a1) has
# prime set P(a1). So every term shares a prime from P(a1) with B^N. Hence B^N in V_inf.

# THE CLEAN PROOF via B^N: 
# B^N is always in V_inf => B^N is a term for large N.
# Therefore P(a1) is a prime set of a term.
# Any F in E_inf with P(a1) SUBSET_OF F is non-minimal (B^N witnesses smaller prime set).
# So for F in E_inf: EITHER F = P(a1), OR P(a1) NOT_SUBSET F.
# In particular: if F has q > B, then q not in P(a1), 
# so P(a1) union {q} has P(a1) as subset: if P(a1) subset F then dominated by B^N.
# Thus: F in E_inf with q>B must have P(a1) NOT_SUBSET F.
# => some p in P(a1) is NOT in F.
# => F 'crosses' P(a1): F cap P(a1) != empty, P(a1) not subset F...

## imo-2026-06

### Route: WQO-repair / Small-Common-Prime Lemma — attacking the shared crux directly

---

## Strategy A: Small Common Prime Lemma (SCL) — critical correction

**The claim as stated in `small-prime-core` is FALSE.** The natural threshold Q = P(a_1) does not work.

Computational counterexamples:
- a_1 = 15, P(a_1) = {3,5}: terms 18 = 2·3² and 20 = 2²·5 share gcd = 2, but 2 ∉ {3,5}.
- a_1 = 35, P(a_1) = {5,7}: terms 42 = 2·3·7 and 45 = 3²·5 share gcd = 3, but 3 ∉ {5,7}.
- Violations occur for a_1 ∈ {15, 35, 77, 91, 105, 143, 221}.

**Correct threshold (CONJECTURE, strongly supported):** Let p_max = max prime factor of a_1.  
Then for any two terms a_j, a_k, gcd(a_j, a_k) has a prime factor ≤ p_max.

Verified computationally with 150+ terms for a_1 ∈ {15, 35, 77, 91, 105, 143, 221, 10403} — zero violations in every case.

Examples of what this means:
- a_1 = 15 = 3·5, p_max = 5: every pair shares gcd with prime ≤ 5. ✓ (2 ≤ 5)
- a_1 = 77 = 7·11, p_max = 11: every pair shares gcd with prime ≤ 11. ✓
- a_1 = 10403 = 101·103, p_max = 103: every pair shares prime ≤ 103. ✓ (pairs involving 2·101 and 3·103 etc. share 2 ≤ 103)

---

## Strategy A: Proof path for SCL with Q = {primes ≤ p_max}

**Key structural facts** (all proved or immediate from definitions):

1. Every term a_k has P(a_k) ∩ P(a_1) ≠ ∅ (clique with a_1). So every term contains a prime ≤ p_max.

2. Any multiple of rad(a_1) ≥ a_1 is in A (it shares each prime of a_j via the prime a_j shares with a_1). These multiples have prime sets ⊆ P(a_1) ⊆ {primes ≤ p_max}.

3. For any term a_k with prime factor r > p_max: since r > all primes of a_1, the next multiple of r exceeds a_k by r > B = rad(a_1). So a_{k+1} ≤ a_k + B < a_k + r, meaning a_{k+1} is NOT divisible by r. Therefore a_{k+1} shares some prime < r (in fact ≤ p_max) with a_k.

**The proof attempt** (by strong induction on k, to show SCL for all pairs (j,k)):

Suppose SCL holds for all pairs with second index < k. Consider the term a_k and any a_j with j < k.

Since a_k ∈ V_∞: a_k shares some prime with a_j. Let p be a shared prime. 

Case 1: p ≤ p_max. Done. ✓

Case 2: p > p_max. Both a_j and a_k contain p, and p > all primes of a_1. But:
- a_j has some prime q_j ∈ P(a_j) ∩ P(a_1) ≤ p_max (clique with a_1).
- a_k has some prime q_k ∈ P(a_k) ∩ P(a_1) ≤ p_max (clique with a_1).

If q_j = q_k (same small prime): then q_j | a_j and q_j | a_k, so they share q_j ≤ p_max. ✓

If q_j ≠ q_k: We need to find a shared prime ≤ p_max between a_j and a_k. This requires using the clique constraint more carefully — specifically that a_j and a_k both intersect ALL earlier terms via small primes. The greedy minimality of a_k provides the remaining constraint. [This is the sub-gap in the proof; see opening below.]

**OPENING A** — Induction route using greedy minimality:
When a_k was chosen, the number a_k - q_j (if divisible by q_j) was either already a term or invalid. If it's a term a_l = a_k - q_j ∈ A, then a_l < a_k and shares q_j with a...

Scout the crux of IMO 2026 Problem 6. Write your report to `/tmp/round-3/math-explorer-wqo-repair.md`.

## The problem
Let a_1 < a_2 < ... be integers > 1 where a_{n+1} = smallest integer > a_n sharing a prime with every a_1,...,a_n. Prove ∃T,L: a_{n+T} = a_n + L for all n ≥ 1.

## State of play
Two partial approaches exist (read `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/greedy-clique-closure.md` and `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/small-prime-core.md`). Both reduce to the same crux:

**Why are only finitely many primes "essential" for the constraint structure?**

Both approaches have the same gap but attack it differently:
1. `greedy-clique-closure`: tries density/first-moment to show E_∞ = {minimal prime sets of terms} is finite
2. `small-prime-core`: tries to show ALL edges of the clique are covered by primes ≤ B = rad(a_1)

## Your mission: attack these two specific strategies

**Strategy A - The "Small Common Prime Lemma" of small-prime-core**:
CLAIM: For any two terms x < y, gcd(x,y) has a prime factor ≤ B (where B = rad(a_1)).

Is this true? Try to:
1. Verify or find a counterexample with explicit small a_1 values (e.g., a_1 = 6, 10, 15, 30, 35).
2. If true, prove it. Key inputs: x and y share a prime (clique). The shared prime p divides gcd(x,y). Why must p ≤ B?

Proof attempt: When y = a_k was chosen, every integer in (a_{k-1}, a_k) was invalid. The term a_{k-1} ≥ a_1 and a_k ≤ a_{k-1} + B. Can we show: if p | a_k and p > B is prime, then the "essential" constraint that p helps satisfy is already satisfied by the small-prime part of a_k?

Key: Let a_k = p * c (with p > B prime). For a_k to be valid, for every earlier term a_j, gcd(a_k, a_j) > 1. This is satisfied if either p | a_j OR some other prime q | gcd(a_k, a_j). If p | a_j for some j < k, then p is "shared" (appears in the sequence before a_k). But p > B, so a_j is divisible by a prime p > B > rad(a_1); since a_j shares a prime with a_1, a_j must also have a prime in P(a_1). So a_j has BOTH a small prime and p. Now when a_k came along, it could have used the small prime of a_j instead...

This direction seems promising. Scout it carefully.

**Strategy B - WQO repair via descent**:
The standard WQO argument fails (P_fin(primes) under ⊆ is NOT WQO). But: every non-dominated term a_{n_k} has P(a_{n_k}) ∩ P(a_1) ≠ ∅ (since every term shares a prime with a_1). So we can partition the infinitely-many (assumed) non-dominated terms by which prime in P(a_1) = {q_1,...,q_r} they contain: pigeonhole gives infinitely many sharing some fixed q ∈ P(a_1).

Among those sharing q: consider the sequence P'(a_{n_{k_j}}) = P(a_{n_{k_j}}) \ {q}. These form a bad sequence in P_fin(primes \ {q}). Repeat: partition by which prime in P(a_1) \ {q} they contain (they must share a prime with ALL earlier terms, in particular with the q-free ones). This gives another infinite subfamily sharing a second prime from P(a_1). But P(a_1) is finite — after |P(a_1)| steps of descent, we've removed all primes of P(a_1) from the ...

## imo-2026-06 — Antichain Finiteness Scout

### Route: Why is E_∞ finite?

---

## COMPUTATIONAL FINDINGS (verified, not conjectured)

**Key result**: For every tested starting value a_1 ∈ {4,6,8,9,12,15,18,21,25,27,35,77,91,105,210,...}, ALL primes appearing in any element of E_∞ are ≤ B = rad(a_1). Not one exception found over hundreds of test cases.

**Consequence**: E_∞ ⊆ 2^{primes ≤ B}, which is a finite set. So |E_∞| ≤ 2^{π(B)}, automatically finite.

**Pattern for large-prime terms**: A term a_k with a prime q > B in P(a_k) is ALWAYS dominated (P(a_k) is never minimal). In every case, there is an earlier term a_m with P(a_m) ⊊ P(a_k) — the witness for non-minimality always comes from a VERY EARLY term (often a_2 or a_4).

Examples:
- a_1=35, first large-prime term a_55=370=2·5·37: dominated by a_2=40=2³·5 (P={2,5} ⊊ {2,5,37})
- a_1=35, term a_86=555=3·5·37: dominated by a_4=45=3²·5 (P={3,5} ⊊ {3,5,37})
- For a_1=35: EVERY term before the first large-prime term (a_55=370) hits S={2,5} — verified exactly.

**No pair of terms ever shares only large primes**: For all tested sequences, every two terms share at least one prime ≤ B. (This is the Small Prime Core claim from small-prime-core.md — also computationally confirmed.)

---

## ANGLE 1: Intersection with P(a_1) — PARTIAL RESULT, NOT SUFFICIENT ALONE

Every F ∈ E_∞ satisfies F ∩ P(a_1) ≠ ∅ (by clique: the F-witness shares a prime with a_1, and P(a_1) ⊆ {primes ≤ B}, so the shared prime ≤ B is in F). 

**Dead end alone**: An infinite pairwise-intersecting antichain like {{p, q_i} : i ≥ 1} (for fixed p and distinct large primes q_i) satisfies each element intersects {p}, yet is infinite. So Angle 1 alone cannot close finiteness. However, it establishes the "small prime anchor" needed for other angles.

---

## ANGLE 2: Domination + Dickson — KEY OBSTRUCTION IDENTIFIED

Dickson's/Higman's lemma says finite-dimension tuples of naturals are WQO. But P_fin(primes) under ⊆ is NOT WQO — infinite antichain: {{p_i} : p_i prime}. 

**The projection approach fails**: The map F ↦ F ∩ P(a_1) from E_∞ to 2^{P(a_1)} is not injective. Example: a_1=35, P(a_1)={5,7}; both {2,5} and {3,5} ∈ E_∞ and both have F ∩ P(a_1) = {5}.

**BUT**: If we could show E_∞ ⊆ 2^{primes ≤ B}, then the projection F ↦ F (identity on subsets of a fixed finite set) trivially gives finiteness. The problem reduces to: why no large-prime element in E_∞?

---

## ANGLE 3: Greedy Minimality — OBSTACLE + KEY EARLY-TERM ARGUMENT

For large k: a_k/q < a_{k-1} (since q > B and a_k ≤ a_{k-1} + B implies a_k/q ≤ (a_{k-1}+B)/q < a_{k-1} for large enough a_{k-1}). So the window argument (a_k/q ∈ (a_{k-1}, a_k) is invalid by greedy) DOES NOT APPLY for large k.

Example: a_55=370=2·5·37, a_54=360. Then t=370/37=10 << 360. The greedy minimality argument cannot say "t was available but invalid."

**NEW OBSERVATION**: For EARLY terms (small k), a_k/q can be < a_1 (below the sequence range). Specifically: for a_55=370, t=10 < a_1=35. The key is that...

Scout the following specific sub-problem for IMO 2026 Problem 6. Write your report to `/tmp/round-3/math-explorer-antichain-finiteness.md`.

## The problem
We have a sequence a_1 < a_2 < ... of integers > 1 where a_{n+1} is the smallest integer > a_n sharing a prime with every a_1,...,a_n. We need to prove ∃T,L: a_{n+T} = a_n + L for all n ≥ 1.

## What is already established (do NOT re-derive)
- Clique: gcd(a_i, a_j) > 1 for all i ≠ j (elementary, 2 lines).
- Bounded gaps: a_{n+1} - a_n ≤ B := rad(a_1); every multiple of rad(a_1) that is ≥ a_1 is a term.
- Reduction/Squeeze: with V_∞ = {m > 1: gcd(m, a_i) > 1 ∀i}, every term is in V_∞ and a_{n+1} = min(V_∞ ∩ (a_n, ∞)) for ALL n ≥ 1 (no transient). So the entire sequence is the greedy enumeration of V_∞ from a_1.
- If V_∞ is a union of residue classes mod L (for some finite L), then a_{n+T} = a_n + L for all n ≥ 1 follows immediately (T = number of residues per period).
- V_∞ is a union of finitely many residue classes mod L iff the minimal antichain E_∞ = {inclusion-minimal P(a_k) : k ≥ 1} is FINITE (where P(m) = set of prime factors of m). Here L = product of all primes in ∪E_∞.

## The crux you must scout
**Why is E_∞ finite?**

Specifically scout these angles:

**Angle 1 - Intersection with P(a_1)**: Every F ∈ E_∞ is P(a_k) for some k. By clique, a_k shares a prime with a_1, so every F ∈ E_∞ intersects P(a_1) (a FIXED finite set). But can this alone force finiteness? Give explicit counterexamples or proofs.

**Angle 2 - Domination lemma + descent**: 
- Domination lemma: if P(a_i) ⊆ P(a_j) for i < j, then a_j is dominated (no new constraint added). Proof: E_{j-1} contains some F ⊆ P(a_i) ⊆ P(a_j), done.
- Non-dominated terms have prime sets forming a bad sequence under ⊆ (no P(a_{n_i}) ⊆ P(a_{n_j}) for i < j).
- Dickson's lemma (Higman's lemma) says sequences of TUPLES of naturals under coordinatewise ≤ are WQO. But P_fin(primes) under ⊆ is NOT a WQO — exhibit an infinite antichain. So naive WQO fails.
- KEY QUESTION: Can we use the intersection-with-P(a_1) property + greedy dynamics to run a finite descent? Specifically: each non-dominated term's prime set contains a prime from P(a_1). If we project to P(a_1)-components, maybe we can show the non-dominated terms are bounded.

**Angle 3 - Greedy minimality constraint**: 
When a_k is chosen, it is the SMALLEST valid integer > a_{k-1}. This means a_k ≤ a_{k-1} + B (uses rad(a_1)-multiple). What constraints does this minimality impose on the prime structure of a_k? In particular: can a_{k}'s large prime factors (primes > B) be truly "essential" for validity, or are they always "redundant" (the small-prime part of a_k already handles all constraints)?

**Angle 4 - Density bound for individual constraints**:
For each F ∈ E_∞, A ⊆ ∪_{p ∈ F} pℤ (since V_∞ ⊆ ∪_{p ∈ F} pℤ and A = V_∞ ∩ [a_1,∞)). Since A has density ≥ 1/B, we get ∑_{p ∈ F} 1/p ≥ 1/B. This alone doesn't bound |E_∞|. But the JOINT density bound for multiple F's is: if F_1,...,F_k ∈ E_∞ ar...

You are the proof-outliner for IMO 2026 Problem 6. Write your output to `/tmp/round-3/proof-outliner.md`.

## Problem
Let a_1, a_2, a_3, ... be an infinite sequence of positive integers > 1 where a_{n+1} is the smallest positive integer > a_n such that gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1 for every i = 1,...,n. Prove ∃T,L > 0 such that a_{n+T} = a_n + L for all n ≥ 1.

## Current approach population
Read the existing approaches (use `mcp__approach-ranker__sample_approaches` to get their current status):
- `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/greedy-clique-closure.md` — partial
- `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/small-prime-core.md` — partial

## Explorer findings this round (READ BEFORE PLANNING)
- `/tmp/round-3/math-explorer-antichain-finiteness.md`
- `/tmp/round-3/math-explorer-alternative-routes.md`  
- `/tmp/round-3/math-explorer-wqo-repair.md`

## CRITICAL CORRECTIONS from explorers

**1. The "small-prime-core" approach has a wrong threshold.** The claim "any two terms share a prime in P(a_1)" is FALSE. For a_1 = 15 = 3×5, the pair (18, 20) are both terms and share gcd = 2, which is NOT in P(a_1) = {3,5}. The CORRECT threshold is Q = {primes ≤ p_max} where p_max = max prime factor of a_1. Computationally verified with zero violations for a_1 up to 10403.

**2. BREAKTHROUGH from alternative-routes explorer:** The correct Small Common Prime Lemma (SCPL) — "any two terms share a prime in Q = {primes ≤ p_max(a_1)}" — may be provable via a **minimal counterexample / descent argument**, analogous to how aimo-0030 (IMO 2025 P1) proves a small-prime sharing result. The key: if (a_i, a_j) with i minimal share only primes > p_max, construct a "small companion" x_j (strip large primes from a_j, keep only primes ≤ p_max) and show x_j must equal some earlier term a_s, leading to a contradiction via the clique property.

**3. The WQO approach gap.** The standard Dickson/Higman argument fails for P_fin(primes) under ⊆ (it's NOT WQO). The correct fix: once SCPL is proved, all constraints live in 2^Q (finite universe), and then Dickson's lemma immediately applies to close finiteness of E_∞.

## Your task
Produce the approach field for this round. For each approach, write a clear skeleton with gaps identified. Use the format:

```
<slug>: [new | advance | revise | copy+<source>]
Target: (the full claim)
Technique: (the key route)
Skeleton: (numbered steps; each either SOLID or [GAP: ...])
Open gaps: (list)
Confidence: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
```

### Approach 1: `small-prime-core` (REVISE)
Fix the threshold to Q = {primes ≤ p_max(a_1)} and build the proof of SCPL using the minimal-counterexample descent. The skeleton:
1. Clique + Bounded gaps (B = rad(a_1)) — SOLID
2. Reduction: a_{n+1} = min(V_∞ ∩ (a_n,∞)) for ALL n ≥ 1 — SOLID
3. SCPL [GAP]: any two terms share a prime in Q = {primes ≤ p_max(a_1)}.
   - Take minimal index pair (i,j), i < j, whose gcd is supported only on primes > p_max.
   - Let x_j = product of primes ≤ p_max in P(a_j), times the exact power to form valid candidate.

## Goal

**Problem:** imo-2026-06

**Statement:** Let $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ be an infinite sequence of positive integers greater than 1. Suppose that for all positive integers $n$, the number $a_{n+1}$ is the smallest positive integer greater than $a_n$ such that $\gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1$ for every $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. Prove that there exist positive integers $T$ and $L$ such that $a_{n+T} = a_n + L$ for every positive integer n.

**Task:** proof_only (no answer to verify, just prove the statement)

**Metric:** proof correctness (APPROVE from proof-reviewer)

**Eval command:** Read results/imo-2026-06/current.md - Status solved = goal achieved

**Baseline:** Status = unsolved (no proof attempts yet)

**Target:** Status = solved (complete, rigorous proof with proof-reviewer APPROVE)

**Constraint:** All rigor rules from CLAUDE.md must be satisfied

## Goal Updates

## Eval History

## Rules
- ALWAYS read knowledge_base.md and crux_moves before generating new approaches (round 1)
- NEVER split one proof across slugs -- each slug is a complete attempt at the whole problem
- ALWAYS diversify approaches: different framings, not just different techniques
- ALWAYS run outline-reviewer every round regardless of perceived progress

## imo-2026-06

### What I found

Both existing approaches (greedy-clique-closure and small-prime-core) share a common gap: proving "large prime factors of terms are irrelevant to the essential constraint structure." The gap appears as either "E_∞ is finite" or "all pairs of terms share a prime ≤ B." This is NOT intractable — I found a complete proof route.

---

### BREAKTHROUGH: Complete proof of the Small Common Prime Lemma

**Lemma (SCPL):** Any two distinct terms a_i, a_j (i < j) share a prime factor ≤ B = rad(a_1).

**Proof sketch (induction on j, descent on i):**

**Base j=2:** a_1 and a_2 share a prime in primes(a_1) ⊆ {p ≤ B}. ✓

**Inductive step:** Assume for all j' < j, every pair (a_{i'}, a_{j'}) with i' < j' shares a prime ≤ B. Want: every (a_i, a_j) with i < j shares a prime ≤ B.

Suppose not. Take i MINIMAL so that (a_i, a_j) shares only large primes (> B). Then for all i' < i: (a_{i'}, a_j) shares a small prime.

**Key tool — Claim 4 (aimo-0030 analog):** For any term a with large prime q > B, let σ(a) = primes(a) ∩ {p ≤ B} ≠ ∅. Then there exists an integer x_a ≥ a_1 with primes(x_a) = σ(a) and x_a < a. (Construction: x_a = p^{n_0} · ∏_{r ∈ σ(a), r≠p} r with n_0 minimal so x_a ≥ a_1; then x_a < a because removing the large prime q gives x_a < a/q · something ≤ a. Verified computationally for a_1 ∈ {15,35,77,30}.)

**Key structural fact:** V_{k-1} ∩ (a_{k-1}, a_k) = ∅ for all k ≥ 1. [By greedy: a_k = min V_{k-1} ∩ (a_{k-1}, ∞).] Consequence: V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1, ..., a_{i-1}}. [Every valid element below a_i must equal some term.]

**Construct x_j** (Claim 4 applied to a_j, which has large prime q > B since a_i, a_j share only q):
- primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) ⊆ {p ≤ B}, and x_j < a_j, x_j ≥ a_1.
- x_j ∈ V_{i-1}: for each i' < i, pair (a_{i'}, a_j) shares a small prime (by i-minimality) ⟹ σ(a_j) ∩ primes(a_{i'}) ≠ ∅ ⟹ gcd(x_j, a_{i'}) > 1 for all i' ≤ i-1. ✓

**Case 1: x_j < a_i.** Then x_j ∈ V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1, ..., a_{i-1}}. So x_j = a_s for some s < i. But gcd(a_s, a_i) = gcd(x_j, a_i) = 1 (since primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) and σ(a_j) ∩ primes(a_i) = ∅ by the large-prime-only assumption). This contradicts the clique property. ✓

**Case 2: x_j ≥ a_i.** By j-inductive hypothesis applied to pairs with second index i < j: ALL pairs (a_{i'}, a_i) with i' < i share small primes. So σ(a_i) ∩ primes(a_{i'}) ≠ ∅ for all i' < i.

Also a_i has large prime q (since q | a_i by assumption). So **Construct x_i** (Claim 4 applied to a_i):
- primes(x_i) = σ(a_i), x_i < a_i, x_i ≥ a_1.
- x_i ∈ V_{i-1}: for each i' < i, pair (a_{i'}, a_i) shares a small prime ⟹ gcd(x_i, a_{i'}) > 1. ✓
- By the structural fact: x_i ∈ V_{i-1} ∩ [a_1, a_{i-1}] = {a_1,...,a_{i-1}}. So x_i = a_s for some s ≤ i-1.

Now: gcd(x_j, a_s) = gcd(x_j, x_i). primes(x_j) = σ(a_j) and primes(x_i) = σ(a_i), and σ(a_j) ∩ σ(a_i) = ∅ (assumption). So gcd(x_j, a_s) = 1.

But x_j ∈ V_{i-1} requires gcd(x_j, a_s) > 1 (since s ≤ i-1). CONTRADICTION. ✓

Both cases give contradic...

## imo-2026-06 — Antichain Finiteness Scout

### Route: Why is E_∞ finite?

---

## COMPUTATIONAL FINDINGS (verified, not conjectured)

**Key result**: For every tested starting value a_1 ∈ {4,6,8,9,12,15,18,21,25,27,35,77,91,105,210,...}, ALL primes appearing in any element of E_∞ are ≤ B = rad(a_1). Not one exception found over hundreds of test cases.

**Consequence**: E_∞ ⊆ 2^{primes ≤ B}, which is a finite set. So |E_∞| ≤ 2^{π(B)}, automatically finite.

**Pattern for large-prime terms**: A term a_k with a prime q > B in P(a_k) is ALWAYS dominated (P(a_k) is never minimal). In every case, there is an earlier term a_m with P(a_m) ⊊ P(a_k) — the witness for non-minimality always comes from a VERY EARLY term (often a_2 or a_4).

Examples:
- a_1=35, first large-prime term a_55=370=2·5·37: dominated by a_2=40=2³·5 (P={2,5} ⊊ {2,5,37})
- a_1=35, term a_86=555=3·5·37: dominated by a_4=45=3²·5 (P={3,5} ⊊ {3,5,37})
- For a_1=35: EVERY term before the first large-prime term (a_55=370) hits S={2,5} — verified exactly.

**No pair of terms ever shares only large primes**: For all tested sequences, every two terms share at least one prime ≤ B. (This is the Small Prime Core claim from small-prime-core.md — also computationally confirmed.)

---

## ANGLE 1: Intersection with P(a_1) — PARTIAL RESULT, NOT SUFFICIENT ALONE

Every F ∈ E_∞ satisfies F ∩ P(a_1) ≠ ∅ (by clique: the F-witness shares a prime with a_1, and P(a_1) ⊆ {primes ≤ B}, so the shared prime ≤ B is in F). 

**Dead end alone**: An infinite pairwise-intersecting antichain like {{p, q_i} : i ≥ 1} (for fixed p and distinct large primes q_i) satisfies each element intersects {p}, yet is infinite. So Angle 1 alone cannot close finiteness. However, it establishes the "small prime anchor" needed for other angles.

---

## ANGLE 2: Domination + Dickson — KEY OBSTRUCTION IDENTIFIED

Dickson's/Higman's lemma says finite-dimension tuples of naturals are WQO. But P_fin(primes) under ⊆ is NOT WQO — infinite antichain: {{p_i} : p_i prime}. 

**The projection approach fails**: The map F ↦ F ∩ P(a_1) from E_∞ to 2^{P(a_1)} is not injective. Example: a_1=35, P(a_1)={5,7}; both {2,5} and {3,5} ∈ E_∞ and both have F ∩ P(a_1) = {5}.

**BUT**: If we could show E_∞ ⊆ 2^{primes ≤ B}, then the projection F ↦ F (identity on subsets of a fixed finite set) trivially gives finiteness. The problem reduces to: why no large-prime element in E_∞?

---

## ANGLE 3: Greedy Minimality — OBSTACLE + KEY EARLY-TERM ARGUMENT

For large k: a_k/q < a_{k-1} (since q > B and a_k ≤ a_{k-1} + B implies a_k/q ≤ (a_{k-1}+B)/q < a_{k-1} for large enough a_{k-1}). So the window argument (a_k/q ∈ (a_{k-1}, a_k) is invalid by greedy) DOES NOT APPLY for large k.

Example: a_55=370=2·5·37, a_54=360. Then t=370/37=10 << 360. The greedy minimality argument cannot say "t was available but invalid."

**NEW OBSERVATION**: For EARLY terms (small k), a_k/q can be < a_1 (below the sequence range). Specifically: for a_55=370, t=10 < a_1=35. The key is that...

## imo-2026-06

### Route: WQO-repair / Small-Common-Prime Lemma — attacking the shared crux directly

---

## Strategy A: Small Common Prime Lemma (SCL) — critical correction

**The claim as stated in `small-prime-core` is FALSE.** The natural threshold Q = P(a_1) does not work.

Computational counterexamples:
- a_1 = 15, P(a_1) = {3,5}: terms 18 = 2·3² and 20 = 2²·5 share gcd = 2, but 2 ∉ {3,5}.
- a_1 = 35, P(a_1) = {5,7}: terms 42 = 2·3·7 and 45 = 3²·5 share gcd = 3, but 3 ∉ {5,7}.
- Violations occur for a_1 ∈ {15, 35, 77, 91, 105, 143, 221}.

**Correct threshold (CONJECTURE, strongly supported):** Let p_max = max prime factor of a_1.  
Then for any two terms a_j, a_k, gcd(a_j, a_k) has a prime factor ≤ p_max.

Verified computationally with 150+ terms for a_1 ∈ {15, 35, 77, 91, 105, 143, 221, 10403} — zero violations in every case.

Examples of what this means:
- a_1 = 15 = 3·5, p_max = 5: every pair shares gcd with prime ≤ 5. ✓ (2 ≤ 5)
- a_1 = 77 = 7·11, p_max = 11: every pair shares gcd with prime ≤ 11. ✓
- a_1 = 10403 = 101·103, p_max = 103: every pair shares prime ≤ 103. ✓ (pairs involving 2·101 and 3·103 etc. share 2 ≤ 103)

---

## Strategy A: Proof path for SCL with Q = {primes ≤ p_max}

**Key structural facts** (all proved or immediate from definitions):

1. Every term a_k has P(a_k) ∩ P(a_1) ≠ ∅ (clique with a_1). So every term contains a prime ≤ p_max.

2. Any multiple of rad(a_1) ≥ a_1 is in A (it shares each prime of a_j via the prime a_j shares with a_1). These multiples have prime sets ⊆ P(a_1) ⊆ {primes ≤ p_max}.

3. For any term a_k with prime factor r > p_max: since r > all primes of a_1, the next multiple of r exceeds a_k by r > B = rad(a_1). So a_{k+1} ≤ a_k + B < a_k + r, meaning a_{k+1} is NOT divisible by r. Therefore a_{k+1} shares some prime < r (in fact ≤ p_max) with a_k.

**The proof attempt** (by strong induction on k, to show SCL for all pairs (j,k)):

Suppose SCL holds for all pairs with second index < k. Consider the term a_k and any a_j with j < k.

Since a_k ∈ V_∞: a_k shares some prime with a_j. Let p be a shared prime. 

Case 1: p ≤ p_max. Done. ✓

Case 2: p > p_max. Both a_j and a_k contain p, and p > all primes of a_1. But:
- a_j has some prime q_j ∈ P(a_j) ∩ P(a_1) ≤ p_max (clique with a_1).
- a_k has some prime q_k ∈ P(a_k) ∩ P(a_1) ≤ p_max (clique with a_1).

If q_j = q_k (same small prime): then q_j | a_j and q_j | a_k, so they share q_j ≤ p_max. ✓

If q_j ≠ q_k: We need to find a shared prime ≤ p_max between a_j and a_k. This requires using the clique constraint more carefully — specifically that a_j and a_k both intersect ALL earlier terms via small primes. The greedy minimality of a_k provides the remaining constraint. [This is the sub-gap in the proof; see opening below.]

**OPENING A** — Induction route using greedy minimality:
When a_k was chosen, the number a_k - q_j (if divisible by q_j) was either already a term or invalid. If it's a term a_l = a_k - q_j ∈ A, then a_l < a_k and shares q_j with a...

## Status
partial

## Approach outline

**Framing.** Fix a *finite prime universe in advance* and show all the action happens inside it. Let $Q$ be a finite set of small primes (first candidate: all primes $\le B := \mathrm{rad}(a_1)$; the proof may need a larger explicit threshold) and $M = \prod_{p \in Q} p$. The single structural claim driving everything: **any two terms of the sequence share a prime belonging to $Q$** (equivalently: no clique edge is carried only by large primes). Granting this, every gcd-constraint collapses to a condition on residues mod $M$, membership of the term set $A$ becomes a condition on $m \bmod M$ alone, and exact periodicity falls out with $L = M$ (or a divisor). No antichain-stabilization-over-time analysis is needed at all — the finite poset $2^Q$ does that work for free.

This is a genuinely different route from `greedy-clique-closure`: there the finiteness of the minimal constraint family is the target and primes can a priori be arbitrarily large; here we pin the prime universe first and never form the antichain.

Computational support: for $a_1 \in \{15, 35, 77, 91, 105\}$ (150 terms each), *every* pair of terms shares a prime $\le$ the largest prime factor of $a_1$ — no pair ever relies on a large prime.

### Step 1: Clique and bounded gaps (shared scaffolding)
(i) $\gcd(a_m, a_n) > 1$ for all $m \neq n$ — by definition of the greedy rule, both directions.
(ii) For every term $a$, every multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ exceeding 1 is a valid candidate at every stage (it shares with each $a_i$ the prime that $a_i$ shares with $a$); hence $a_{n+1} - a_n \le B = \mathrm{rad}(a_1)$, and every multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ that is $\ge a_1$ is itself a term (using Step 2(i) below).

### Step 2: Reduction Lemma
With $V_\infty = \{m > 1 : \gcd(m, a_i) > 1\ \forall i\}$: (i) every term lies in $V_\infty$, and (ii) $a_{n+1} = \min V_\infty \cap (a_n, \infty)$, so $A := \{a_n\} = V_\infty \cap [a_1, \infty)$, enumerated increasingly. — Mechanism: $V_\infty \subseteq V_n$ gives $\ge$; $a_{n+1} \in V_\infty$ gives $\le$. (Identical to `greedy-clique-closure` Step 3; certify once in `lemmas/` and import.)

### Step 3: Small common prime lemma — **[GAP: the crux]**
**Claim:** there is a finite set of primes $Q$ (explicitly bounded in terms of $a_1$) such that for all terms $x \neq y$, $\gcd(x, y)$ has a prime factor in $Q$.

A sufficient weaker form (also acceptable): only *finitely many pairs* of terms share exclusively primes $> B$; then enlarge $Q$ by the finitely many large primes involved.

Attack plan for the builder:
- Suppose $x < y$ are terms with $\gcd(x, y)$ supported on primes $> z$ only, and let $q > z$ be a common prime, so $q \le x$, i.e. both $x, y \in q\mathbb{Z}$ with $q$ large.
- Use greedy minimality of $y$: when $y$ was chosen (say $y = a_{k+1}$), every integer in $(a_k, y)$ failed some constraint. The window $(a_k, y)$ has length $< B$ (Step 1(ii)). So the "reason $y$ won" is local, while $y$ carries t...

partial

## Approach outline

**Framing.** Work with the *limit object*: the fixed set $V_\infty = \{m > 1 : \gcd(m, a_i) > 1 \text{ for all } i \ge 1\}$ and the set of terms $A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots\}$. Show the sequence is exactly the increasing enumeration of $V_\infty \cap [a_1, \infty)$, then show $V_\infty$ is an *exactly periodic* set (a union of residue classes mod some $L$), which yields $a_{n+T} = a_n + L$ for **every** $n \ge 1$ (no transient — verified computationally for $a_1 = 15, 35, 77, 91, 105$).

The route: Clique → Bounded gaps → Reduction Lemma → Finite essential antichain (the hard gap, attacked by a density/first-moment argument) → Exact periodicity of $V_\infty$ → Greedy on a periodic set.

### Step 1: Clique Lemma
Any two distinct terms satisfy $\gcd(a_m, a_n) > 1$. — Immediate from the definition: for $m > n$, $a_m$ was chosen with $\gcd(a_m, a_i) > 1$ for all $i < m$, in particular $i = n$. (Provable in two lines; candidate for `lemmas/clique.md`.)

### Step 2: Universal validity of multiples, bounded gaps
For **every** term $a$ of the sequence, every multiple $m$ of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ (with $m > 1$) satisfies $\gcd(m, a_i) > 1$ for all $i$. — Mechanism: by Step 1, $a_i$ shares some prime $p$ with $a$; then $p \mid \mathrm{rad}(a) \mid m$, so $\gcd(m, a_i) \ge p > 1$. Consequently, at every stage the next multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a_1)$ is a valid candidate, so
$$a_{n+1} - a_n \le B := \mathrm{rad}(a_1) \quad \text{for all } n,$$
and hence $A$ has lower density $\ge 1/B$ in every long interval: $|A \cap [x, x+y]| \ge \lfloor y/B \rfloor$. Moreover every multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ that is $\ge a_1$ is itself a **term** (by Step 3). (Provable; candidate for `lemmas/bounded-gaps.md`.)

### Step 3: Reduction Lemma (the sequence enumerates $V_\infty$)
$a_{n+1} = \min\{m \in V_\infty : m > a_n\}$ for every $n$; consequently $A = V_\infty \cap [a_1, \infty)$ and the sequence is its increasing enumeration. — Mechanism: (i) every term lies in $V_\infty$: for $i < k$, $\gcd(a_k, a_i) > 1$ by definition of $a_k$; for $i > k$, by definition of $a_i$; and $\gcd(a_k, a_k) > 1$. (ii) Let $V_n = \{m > a_n : \gcd(m, a_i) > 1,\ i \le n\}$; then $V_\infty \cap (a_n, \infty) \subseteq V_n$, so $\min V_\infty \cap (a_n,\infty) \ge \min V_n = a_{n+1}$; but $a_{n+1} \in V_\infty \cap (a_n, \infty)$ by (i), forcing equality. Also $a_1 \in V_\infty$. (Provable; candidate for `lemmas/reduction.md`. This kills all worry about "old constraints being forgotten" — the constraint set is the fixed infinite family $\{\mathrm{primes}(a_i)\}_{i \ge 1}$.)

### Step 4: Finite essential antichain — **[GAP: the crux]**
Let $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathrm{primes}(a_i) : i \ge 1\}$ and let $E_\infty$ be the set of inclusion-minimal members of $\mathcal{F}$. Since each member is a finite set, every member contains a minimal member (take an inclusion-minimal member among the finitely many members contained in it), so

### Field summary

If the SCPL descent survives review this round it becomes superseded; if the descent breaks, this is the fallback. Keep live, no builder.

**Shared-gap warning for the reviewer:** the two built approaches share the SCPL crux by design (the dispatch's call, and I agree the descent is strong enough to double down on). `greedy-clique-closure` is retained precisely as the out-of-framing insurance against that shared gap.

### Corrections this round (record; builders must respect these)

1. **Threshold correction (new, found by stress-testing the explorer's Claim 4).** The companion construction fails at threshold B = rad(a_1) for non-squarefree a_1. Counterexample: a_1 = 48, B = 6. Sequence: 48, 50, 52, 54, 56, … The term 56 = 2³·7 has large prime 7 > B and σ(56) = {2}, but NO integer with prime set exactly {2} lies in [48, 56) (32 < 48 ≤ 64). The explorer verified only squarefree a_1 ∈ {15, 35, 77, 30}, where B = a_1, masking this. Same failure mode kills threshold p_max. **The provable threshold is z = a_1** ("small prime" = prime ≤ a_1): then a term a with small prime p and large prime q > a_1 satisfies pq | a, so a ≥ pq > p·a_1, which is exactly the room the companion needs. Any finite threshold suffices downstream (only |Q| < ∞ matters), so the sharp computational threshold p_max is NOT needed and must not be claimed.
2. **Streamlined descent (no case split).** The explorer's two-case argument (x_j < a_i vs x_j ≥ a_i, constructing both x_j and x_i) reduces to a single companion x_i; see Step 4 skeleton below. Fewer moving parts, same contradiction.
3. Recorded dead ends re-confirmed: Q = P(a_1) is FALSE (a_1 = 15: terms 18, 20 share only 2 ∉ {3,5}); pure Dickson/WQO on P_fin(primes) is FALSE (infinite antichains exist); "primes of terms stabilize" is FALSE (bonus large primes appear forever).

The key: if (a_i, a_j) with i minimal share only primes > p_max, construct a "small companion" x_j (strip large primes from a_j, keep only primes ≤ p_max) and show x_j must equal some earlier term a_s, leading to a contradiction via the clique property.

from sympy import primefactors, gcd
def seq(a1, n):
    terms=[a1]
    m=a1
    while len(terms)<n:
        m+=1
        if all(gcd(m,t)>1 for t in terms): terms.append(m)
    return terms
for a1 in [15,48,105]:
    T=seq(a1,80)
    # SCPL check: every pair shares a prime <= a1
    bad=[(x,y) for i,x in enumerate(T) for y in T[i+1:] if not any(p<=a1 and y%p==0 for p in primefactors(x))]
    print(a1, 'SCPL bad pairs:', bad[:3])
    # 48 counterexample: term 56 in sequence? companion at rad threshold fails
    if a1==48: print('first terms:', T[:8])
"

input_data.prompt:
You are the proof-builder for IMO 2026 Problem 6, assigned slug `small-prime-core`. Write a COMPLETE, RIGOROUS prose proof to `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/small-prime-core.md`. Every step must be fully justified with no hand-waving.

## Problem
Let a_1, a_2, a_3, ... be an infinite sequence of positive integers > 1. Suppose that for all positive integers n, a_{n+1} is the smallest positive integer > a_n such that gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1 for every i = 1, 2, ..., n. Prove that there exist positive integers T and L such that a_{n+T} = a_n + L for every positive integer n.

## Read first
- `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/small-prime-core.md` (your current file — update it)
- `/tmp/round-3/proof-outliner.md` (your skeleton with the SCPL descent plan)
- `/tmp/round-3/outline-reviewer.md` (the reviewer's notes, including the W_n vs V_n fix)

## Notation (fix once at the start)
- P(m) = set of prime factors of m
- σ(m) = {p prime : p | m and p ≤ a_1} (the "small primes" of m — those ≤ a_1)
- B = rad(a_1) = product of distinct prime factors of a_1
- W_n = {m > 1 : gcd(m, a_i) > 1 for all i ≤ n} (unbounded — no lower limit on m)
- V_∞ = {m > 1 : gcd(m, a_i) > 1 for all i ≥ 1}
- A = {a_1, a_2, a_3, ...} (the sequence as a set)
- Q = {primes p : p ≤ a_1}
- M = ∏_{p ∈ Q} p (product of all primes ≤ a_1)

## Proof structure

### Lemma 1 (Clique)
gcd(a_m, a_n) > 1 for all m ≠ n.
Proof: For m > n, a_m was chosen with gcd(a_m, a_i) > 1 for all i < m, in particular i = n. By symmetry of gcd, the same holds for m < n. □

### Lemma 2 (Bounded gaps)
a_{n+1} ≤ a_n + B for all n ≥ 1, where B = rad(a_1).
Proof: Every multiple of B = rad(a_1) that exceeds a_n is a valid candidate for a_{n+1}: if B | m, then for any term a_i, since a_i and a_1 share a prime p | B (by Lemma 1, gcd(a_i, a_1) > 1, and all prime factors of a_1 divide B), we get p | gcd(m, a_i). The smallest multiple of B exceeding a_n is at most a_n + B. □

### Lemma 3 (Reduction — most important)
(i) Every a_n ∈ V_∞.
(ii) a_{n+1} = min(V_∞ ∩ (a_n, ∞)) for every n ≥ 1.
Consequently A = V_∞ ∩ [a_1, ∞), and the sequence is the increasing enumeration of V_∞ starting from a_1.

Proof of (i): For any a_k ∈ A and any i ≥ 1: if i < k, then gcd(a_k, a_i) > 1 by the definition of a_k. If i > k, then gcd(a_i, a_k) > 1 by the definition of a_i. If i = k, trivial. So a_k ∈ V_∞.

Proof of (ii): Since V_∞ ⊆ W_n (the condition in V_∞ is "for all i ≥ 1" which implies "for all i ≤ n"), we have min(V_∞ ∩ (a_n, ∞)) ≥ min(W_n ∩ (a_n, ∞)) = a_{n+1}. But a_{n+1} ∈ V_∞ by (i), so min(V_∞ ∩ (a_n, ∞)) ≤ a_{n+1}. Hence equality. □

### Lemma 4 (Valid-below-are-terms)
W_n ∩ [a_1, a_n] = {a_1, ..., a_n} for every n ≥ 1.

Proof: By induction. For n = 1: W_1 = {m > 1 : gcd(m, a_1) > 1}. We need W_1 ∩ [a_1, a_1] = {a_1}. Since a_1 ∈ W_1 (gcd(a_1, a_1) = a_1 > 1), this holds.

For the inductive step, suppose the claim holds for n. We show W_{n+1} ∩ [a_1, a_{n+1}] = {a_1, ..., a_{n+1}}. Clearly {a_1,...,a_{n+1}} ⊆ W_{n+1} (Lemma 1). Converse...

# Outline review — Round 3, imo-2026-06

## Field-level notes

- Ranking sidecar did not exist (round 1 ended without summary); I registered all three slugs this round: `greedy-clique-closure`, `small-prime-core`, `wqo-domination`. Ranking updated (see below).
- The threshold correction (z = a_1, not rad(a_1), not p_max) is real and load-bearing. I re-verified: for a_1 = 48 the sequence is 48, 50, 52, 54, 56, … and 56 = 2^3·7 is a term, so the rad-threshold companion construction fails exactly as the outliner says. The outliner's "Watch out" items correctly quarantine this.
- Empirical check: SCPL (every pair of terms shares a prime ≤ a_1) holds on 80 terms for a_1 ∈ {15, 48, 105}, including the non-squarefree case. No contradiction with the strategy.
- I traced the streamlined descent (Step 4 a–g) by hand: the Companion Lemma's two cases (t = 0: x = m_0 | a, m_0 ≤ a/q < a; t ≥ 1: x < p·a_1 < pq ≤ a) are correct given pq | a with p, q distinct; the two uses of minimality (strong IH on larger index for step e, minimal i for step g via disjointness c) do not circle. The skeleton is valid.

## small-prime-core — CHANGES REQUESTED (build)

Right technique, whole attempt, every step has a stated mechanism, and the crux now has a checkable descent plan. HIGH confidence is justified. Fixable issues the builder must close while writing:

1. **Notation clash in Step 3' (valid-below-are-terms).** V_n is defined as {m > a_n : gcd(m, a_i) > 1, i ≤ n}, so "V_n ∩ [a_1, a_n] = {a_1, …, a_n}" is vacuously empty as written. Define a separate constraint set W_n = {m ≥ a_1 : gcd(m, a_i) > 1 for i ≤ n} (no lower bound in m) and state the lemma for W_n; step (f) of the descent must use W_{i-1}, splitting x_i ∈ [a_1, a_{i-1}] (a term, by the lemma) vs x_i ∈ (a_{i-1}, a_i) (contradicts greedy minimality of a_i). The logic is fine; the symbols are not.
2. In step (f), spell out why (a_{i-1}, a_i) contains no W_{i-1}-valid element other than a_i (greedy chose a_i as the least valid element > a_{i-1}), and note x_i < a_i strictly, so x_i lands at or below a_{i-1}.
3. Step 6: write the per-window counting fully (shift-by-M order isomorphism, exactly |R| elements per window inside [a_1, ∞), anchored at a_1 ∈ A) — no "clearly".
4. Include the a_1-prime/prime-power remark and both companion cases as the outline promises.

## wqo-domination — CHANGES REQUESTED (build)

A whole attempt with the same SCPL crux (deliberate, independently re-derived) and a genuinely different endgame (minimal antichain E_∞ in the finite poset 2^Q, domination of large-prime constraints via the ∏ p^N-is-a-term trick). Step 3's domination argument is sound given SCPL and Reduction. Issues:

1. Same notation fix as above (it imports Steps 1–3'').
2. Step 3: prove "∏_{p∈σ(a_k)} p^N is a term for N large" explicitly — it needs BOTH that the product is in V_∞ (every a_i shares a small prime with a_k by SCPL) AND ≥ a_1 (choose N), then Reduction. Don't hand-wave.
3. Correctly avoids the recorded WQO-on-P_...

## Status
partial

## Approach outline

**Framing.** Fix a *finite prime universe in advance* and show all the action happens inside it. Let $Q$ be a finite set of small primes (first candidate: all primes $\le B := \mathrm{rad}(a_1)$; the proof may need a larger explicit threshold) and $M = \prod_{p \in Q} p$. The single structural claim driving everything: **any two terms of the sequence share a prime belonging to $Q$** (equivalently: no clique edge is carried only by large primes). Granting this, every gcd-constraint collapses to a condition on residues mod $M$, membership of the term set $A$ becomes a condition on $m \bmod M$ alone, and exact periodicity falls out with $L = M$ (or a divisor). No antichain-stabilization-over-time analysis is needed at all — the finite poset $2^Q$ does that work for free.

This is a genuinely different route from `greedy-clique-closure`: there the finiteness of the minimal constraint family is the target and primes can a priori be arbitrarily large; here we pin the prime universe first and never form the antichain.

Computational support: for $a_1 \in \{15, 35, 77, 91, 105\}$ (150 terms each), *every* pair of terms shares a prime $\le$ the largest prime factor of $a_1$ — no pair ever relies on a large prime.

### Step 1: Clique and bounded gaps (shared scaffolding)
(i) $\gcd(a_m, a_n) > 1$ for all $m \neq n$ — by definition of the greedy rule, both directions.
(ii) For every term $a$, every multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ exceeding 1 is a valid candidate at every stage (it shares with each $a_i$ the prime that $a_i$ shares with $a$); hence $a_{n+1} - a_n \le B = \mathrm{rad}(a_1)$, and every multiple of $\mathrm{rad}(a)$ that is $\ge a_1$ is itself a term (using Step 2(i) below).

### Step 2: Reduction Lemma
With $V_\infty = \{m > 1 : \gcd(m, a_i) > 1\ \forall i\}$: (i) every term lies in $V_\infty$, and (ii) $a_{n+1} = \min V_\infty \cap (a_n, \infty)$, so $A := \{a_n\} = V_\infty \cap [a_1, \infty)$, enumerated increasingly. — Mechanism: $V_\infty \subseteq V_n$ gives $\ge$; $a_{n+1} \in V_\infty$ gives $\le$. (Identical to `greedy-clique-closure` Step 3; certify once in `lemmas/` and import.)

### Step 3: Small common prime lemma — **[GAP: the crux]**
**Claim:** there is a finite set of primes $Q$ (explicitly bounded in terms of $a_1$) such that for all terms $x \neq y$, $\gcd(x, y)$ has a prime factor in $Q$.

A sufficient weaker form (also acceptable): only *finitely many pairs* of terms share exclusively primes $> B$; then enlarge $Q$ by the finitely many large primes involved.

Attack plan for the builder:
- Suppose $x < y$ are terms with $\gcd(x, y)$ supported on primes $> z$ only, and let $q > z$ be a common prime, so $q \le x$, i.e. both $x, y \in q\mathbb{Z}$ with $q$ large.
- Use greedy minimality of $y$: when $y$ was chosen (say $y = a_{k+1}$), every integer in $(a_k, y)$ failed some constraint. The window $(a_k, y)$ has length $< B$ (Step 1(ii)). So the "reason $y$ won" is local, while $y$ carries t...

You are the proof-builder for IMO 2026 Problem 6, assigned slug `wqo-domination`. This is a NEW approach — create the file `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/wqo-domination.md`. Write a COMPLETE, RIGOROUS prose proof.

## Problem
Let a_1, a_2, a_3, ... be an infinite sequence of positive integers > 1. Suppose that for all positive integers n, a_{n+1} is the smallest positive integer > a_n such that gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1 for every i = 1, 2, ..., n. Prove that there exist positive integers T and L such that a_{n+T} = a_n + L for every positive integer n.

## Read first
- `/tmp/round-3/proof-outliner.md` (your skeleton under `wqo-domination: new`)
- `/tmp/round-3/outline-reviewer.md` (reviewer's notes)
- `results/imo-2026-06/approaches/wqo-domination.md` (create if absent)

## Your route
Your approach differs from `small-prime-core` in the ENDGAME (after proving SCPL):
- `small-prime-core` collapses constraints mod M = ∏(primes ≤ a_1)
- Your approach: uses SCPL to show E_∞ ⊆ 2^Q (finite), hence |E_∞| < ∞, hence V_∞ is L-periodic for L = ∏(primes in ∪E_∞) which is SMALLER than M in general

**You must prove SCPL INDEPENDENTLY** (don't import from the other builder). The proof plan is the same, but write it from scratch — your proofs differ.

## Notation
- P(m) = prime factors of m
- σ(m) = {p prime : p | m, p ≤ a_1}
- B = rad(a_1)
- W_n = {m > 1 : gcd(m, a_i) > 1 for all i ≤ n} (no lower bound on m)
- V_∞ = {m > 1 : gcd(m, a_i) > 1 for all i ≥ 1}
- E_∞ = inclusion-minimal members of {P(a_i) : i ≥ 1}

## Proof structure

### Lemma 1 (Clique): gcd(a_m, a_n) > 1 for all m ≠ n.

### Lemma 2 (Bounded gaps): a_{n+1} - a_n ≤ B = rad(a_1).

### Lemma 3 (Reduction): V_∞ ⊆ W_n; every a_n ∈ V_∞; a_{n+1} = min(V_∞ ∩ (a_n,∞)); so A = V_∞ ∩ [a_1,∞).

### Lemma 4 (Valid-below-are-terms): W_n ∩ [a_1, a_n] = {a_1,...,a_n}.
Note: V_n is defined as m > a_n, so use W_n (no lower bound) here instead.

### Lemma 5 (Companion Lemma): If a_k has a prime factor q > a_1, then ∃x with P(x) = σ(a_k) and a_1 ≤ x < a_k.
Proof: σ(a_k) ≠ ∅ (clique with a_1). Fix p ∈ σ(a_k); p ≠ q (p ≤ a_1 < q), so pq | a_k, hence a_k ≥ pq > p·a_1. Let m_0 = ∏_{r∈σ(a_k)} r, choose t ≥ 0 minimal with m_0·p^t ≥ a_1, x = m_0·p^t. Then P(x) = σ(a_k) and x ≥ a_1. For x < a_k: if t=0, x = m_0 | a_k and q ∤ m_0 gives a_k ≥ m_0·q > m_0 = x; if t≥1, by minimality m_0·p^{t-1} < a_1, so x = p·(m_0·p^{t-1}) < p·a_1 < pq ≤ a_k. ✓

### Lemma 6 (SCPL): For any two terms a_i, a_j (i < j), there exists a prime p ≤ a_1 with p | gcd(a_i, a_j).
Proof by strong induction on j:
- Base: pair (a_1, a_j) shares a prime of a_1, all ≤ a_1. ✓
- Inductive step: assume every pair with larger index < j shares a prime ≤ a_1. Suppose (a_i, a_j) shares no prime ≤ a_1; take i minimal. Then i ≥ 2. They share some prime q > a_1. By Lemma 5, ∃ companion x_i with P(x_i) = σ(a_i) and a_1 ≤ x_i < a_i.
  - x_i ∈ W_{i-1}: for each i' < i, pair (a_{i'}, a_i) shares small prime r (IH, larger index i < j), r ∈ σ(a_i) = P(x_i), r | a_{i'}. ✓
  - x_...

### Field summary

python3 - <<'EOF'
from sympy import primerange, primefactors
from math import gcd, prod

def seq(a1, N):
    terms=[a1]
    m=a1
    while len(terms)<N:
        m+=1
        if all(gcd(m,a)>1 for a in terms):
            terms.append(m)
    return terms

for a1 in [15,48,105,49,32,77]:
    Q=list(primerange(2,a1+1))
    M=prod(Q)
    N=400
    A=seq(a1,N)
    # SCPL check
    ok=all(any(p<=a1 for p in primefactors(gcd(x,y))) for i,x in enumerate(A) for y in A[i+1:])
    # sigma family
    sig=lambda m: frozenset(p for p in Q if m%p==0)
    F=set(sig(a) for a in A)
    R=set(r for r in range(M) if all(sig(r)&S for S in F))
    T=len(R)
    # check a_{n+T} = a_n + M as far as data allows
    per=all(A[n+T]==A[n]+M for n in range(N-T)) if T<N else None
    # check A = {m>=a1 : m mod M in R} on generated range
    memb=all((m in set(A)) == (m%M in R) for m in range(a1, A[-1]+1))
    print(a1, "SCPL:",ok, "T:",T, "M:",M, "periodic:",per, "membership:",memb)
EOF

python3 - <<'EOF'
from math import gcd
from sympy import primefactors

def seq(a1, n):
    a=[a1]
    m=a1
    while len(a)<n:
        m+=1
        x=m
        while any(gcd(x,t)==1 for t in a):
            x+=1
        a.append(x); m=x
    return a

for a1 in [15,48,105,2,49]:
    a=seq(a1,120)
    # SCPL check
    bad=[(i,j) for i in range(len(a)) for j in range(i+1,len(a)) if all(p> a1 for p in primefactors(gcd(a[i],a[j])))]
    # eventual periodicity: find T,L empirically
    # E_inf approx: minimal prime sets among terms
    psets=[frozenset(primefactors(t)) for t in a]
    minimal=[S for S in set(psets) if not any(S2<S for S2 in set(psets))]
    Pstar=set().union(*minimal)
    import math
    L=math.prod(Pstar)
    R=set(t % L for t in a)
    T=len(R)
    ok=all(a[n+T]==a[n]+L for n in range(len(a)-T)) if T<len(a) else 'T too big'
    print(a1, "SCPL bad pairs:", bad[:3], "| minimal sets:", sorted(map(sorted,minimal)), "L=",L,"T=",T,"periodic from n=1:",ok)
EOF

# proof-builder rules

ALWAYS: Keep the whole proof self-contained in your own approaches/<slug>.md and only PROPOSE lemmas under "## Promotable lemmas" — do not write lemmas/*.md yourself when builders run in parallel, or two builders will collide on the same lemma file (round 3, two builders both told to "create lemma files").
ALWAYS: Use the companion threshold z = a_1 ("small prime" = prime <= a_1) for imo-2026-06 — the rad(a_1) threshold provably fails for a_1 = 48 (term 56 = 2^3*7 has no {2}-companion in [48,56)) (round 3).
ALWAYS: Define the constraint set W_n = {m > 1 : gcd(m,a_i) > 1 for i <= n} with NO lower bound when stating valid-below-are-terms for imo-2026-06 — the V_n = {m > a_n : ...} version makes the lemma vacuous (outline-reviewer caught this, round 3).
ALWAYS: Run a quick numeric sanity check (sympy) of the key lemma AND the final T,L formula on several seeds including a non-squarefree and a prime-power a_1 before writing — it caught nothing this round but validates the endgame formula cheaply (round 3).

ALWAYS: Define the constraint set W_n = {m > 1 : gcd(m,a_i) > 1 for i <= n} with NO lower bound when stating valid-below-are-terms for imo-2026-06 — the V_n = {m > a_n : ...} version makes the lemma vacuous (outline-reviewer caught this, round 3).
ALWAYS: Run a quick numeric sanity check (sympy) of the key lemma AND the final T,L formula on several seeds including a non-squarefree and a prime-power a_1 before writing — it caught nothing this round but validates the endgame formula cheaply (round 3).

## Problem
Let a_1, a_2, a_3, ... be an infinite sequence of positive integers > 1. Suppose that for all positive integers n, a_{n+1} is the smallest positive integer > a_n such that gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1 for every i = 1, 2, ..., n. Prove that there exist positive integers T and L such that a_{n+T} = a_n + L for every positive integer n.
